title: The quest to kill Gmail finds a new AI challenger
excerpt: Scape's email app draws on its memory and transcripts to suggest replies. A Y Combinator partner believes it could finally pry people away from Gmail.
content_html:
Exclusive

By Melia Russell

You're currently following this author! Want to unfollow? Unsubscribe via the link in your email.

For decades, email has been where work piles up. Now, two 23-year-old Swedes are asking: What if the inbox could start checking things off?

That's the vision behind Scape, a desktop email app that wants to do the work buried inside messages — and one that Y Combinator partner Gustaf Alströmer believes could finally pry people away from Gmail.

The Stockholm startup is opening a waitlist for early access on Thursday after raising $3.2 million from Y Combinator, General Catalyst, FundersClub, and other investors, Business Insider has learned. New users will be admitted in batches.

"It's painfully obvious that AI will transform email," Alströmer said in a statement. "Once people start using the product, they just don't go back to Gmail."

That is a bold promise in a category littered with beautifully designed casualties. Startups have spent years trying to outmaneuver Gmail with faster search, cleaner design, and clever keyboard shortcuts. Scape founder Melvin Hagberg argues they were all constrained by the technology of their time: There was only so much anyone could change about the basic job of an email client.

AI agents — systems that can take actions on a user's behalf — blew open what was possible, Hagberg said. Scape does not want to help people write emails faster. It wants to handle whatever the email asks them to do.

In a demo for Business Insider, Hagberg opened an email from a prospective customer asking whether to join Scape's waitlist or book a call. Scape drew on its memory of his past messages and surfaced four possible responses. When Hagberg chose the call option, the app drafted a reply with a proposed time based on his calendar.

Scape can also draw on transcripts from its built-in meeting recorder to remember what was discussed. In another example, the app found an order form, filled in the blanks for a particular customer, attached it to a response, and left the finished email for Hagberg to review.

Scape does not send the message itself. Hagberg said the app is designed to keep the user in control, particularly when an agent is making decisions based on years of private messaging.

Users can also instruct Scape through a chatbot built into the app. They can ask it to create a label to track emails for a project, draft variations of the same message for a group of recipients, or remember how they prefer to handle similar emails in the future.

Scape plans to make money through subscriptions, though the company has not disclosed pricing.

Hagberg arrived at the idea by creating a similar system for a much narrower group of workers.

After leaving his job as head of technology at an e-commerce startup, he began building software for customer support agents — the human kind. The tool helped them move beyond answering tickets and take actions across the other systems they used.

In 2025, Hagberg entered Y Combinator. There, he began to see that the same idea could solve many of his own frustrations with email. He pivoted the company to serve a much larger market.

Scape now has a second cofounder, Elis Hodzic, and three employees, all based in Stockholm. It has attracted backing from Swedish tech royalty, including Sebastian Knutsson, a cofounder of the game-maker behind Candy Crush, and Max Junestrand, whose startup Legora has become one of legal tech's fastest-growing companies. Other angel investors include employees from OpenAI, Google, Meta, and Ramp.

Scape is entering a crowded market. Superhuman has built a cult following around speed, while Google is adding Gemini features directly to Gmail.

Hagberg argues that those apps still help people manage email.

Scape's bet is that it can loosen Google's grip by doing the work inside the inbox.

Read next

Melia Russell

sentences.en: Exclusive
sentences.zh: 独家的

content_html_zh:
独家的

作者：Melia Russell 您目前正在关注该作者！ 想要取消关注吗？ 通过电子邮件中的链接取消订阅。

几十年来，电子邮件一直是工作堆积的地方。 现在，两名 23 岁的瑞典人问：如果收件箱可以开始核对邮件会怎么样？

这就是 Scape 背后的愿景，这是一款桌面电子邮件应用程序，希望完成隐藏在消息中的工作，Y Combinator 合作伙伴 Gustaf Alströmer 认为该应用程序最终可以将人们从 Gmail 中夺走。

据 Business Insider 获悉，这家斯德哥尔摩初创公司在从 Y Combinator、General Catalyst、FundersClub 和其他投资者筹集了 320 万美元后，将于周四开放抢先体验等候名单。 新用户将分批接纳。

阿尔斯特罗默在一份声明中表示：“显而易见，人工智能将改变电子邮件。” “一旦人们开始使用该产品，他们就不会再回到 Gmail。”

对于一个充斥着设计精美的伤亡的类别来说，这是一个大胆的承诺。 多年来，初创公司一直试图通过更快的搜索、更简洁的设计和巧妙的键盘快捷键来战胜 Gmail。 Scape 创始人梅尔文·哈格伯格 (Melvin Hagberg) 认为，他们都受到了当时技术的限制：对于电子邮件客户端的基本工作，任何人都只能做出如此多的改变。

哈格伯格说，人工智能代理——可以代表用户采取行动的系统——开启了可能性。 Scape 不想帮助人们更快地写电子邮件。 它想要处理电子邮件要求他们做的任何事情。

在 Business Insider 的演示中，Hagberg 打开了一封来自潜在客户的电子邮件，询问是否加入 Scape 的候补名单或预约电话。 Scape 利用其对他过去消息的记忆，提出了四种可能的回应。 当哈格伯格选择通话选项时，该应用程序会根据他的日历起草一份回复，其中包含建议的时间。

Scape 还可以利用其内置会议记录器中的记录来记住讨论的内容。 在另一个示例中，该应用程序找到了一份订单，填写了特定客户的空白，将其附加到回复中，并将完成的电子邮件留给 Hagberg 进行审核。

Scape 本身并不发送消息。 哈格伯格表示，该应用程序旨在让用户保持控制，特别是当代理根据多年的私人消息做出决策时。

用户还可以通过应用程序内置的聊天机器人来指导 Scape。 他们可以要求它创建一个标签来跟踪项目的电子邮件，为一组收件人起草同一消息的变体，或者记住他们将来喜欢如何处理类似的电子邮件。

Scape 计划通过订阅来赚钱，但该公司尚未透露定价。

哈格伯格通过为一小部分工人创建了一个类似的系统来提出这个想法。

在辞去一家电子商务初创公司技术主管的职务后，他开始为人类客户支持代理构建软件。 该工具帮助他们超越了回答问题的范围，并在他们使用的其他系统上采取行动。

2025 年，哈格伯格进入 Y Combinator。 在那里，他开始发现同样的想法可以解决他自己在电子邮件方面遇到的许多挫折。 他将公司的重心转向服务更大的市场。

Scape 现在有第二位联合创始人 Elis Hodzic 和三名员工，他们都住在斯德哥尔摩。 它吸引了瑞典科技巨头的支持，其中包括《Candy Crush》游戏开发商联合创始人 Sebastian Knutsson 和 Max Junestrand，他的初创公司 Legora 已成为法律科技发展最快的公司之一。 其他天使投资人包括来自 OpenAI、Google、Meta 和 Ramp 的员工。

Scape 正在进入一个拥挤的市场。 Super human 凭借速度赢得了狂热的追随者，而 Google 则将 Gemini 功能直接添加到 Gmail 中。

哈格伯格认为，这些应用程序仍然可以帮助人们管理电子邮件。

Scape 的赌注是，它可以通过在收件箱内进行工作来放松谷歌的控制。
